DuplicateHandle

The DuplicateHandle function duplicates an object handle. The duplicate handle refers to the same object as the original handle. Therefore, any changes to the object are reflected through both handles. For example, the current file mark for a file handle is always the same for both handles. 

BOOL DuplicateHandle(
  HANDLE hSourceProcessHandle,  // handle to source process
  HANDLE hSourceHandle,         // handle to duplicate
  HANDLE hTargetProcessHandle,  // handle to target process
  LPHANDLE lpTargetHandle,      // duplicate handle
  DWORD dwDesiredAccess,        // requested access
  BOOL bInheritHandle,          // handle inheritance option
  DWORD dwOptions               // optional actions
);

Parameters

hSourceProcessHandle
[in] Ignored.
hSourceHandle
[in] Handle to duplicate. This must be a valid open object handle. For a list of objects whose handles can be duplicated, see the following Remarks section.
hTargetProcessHandle
[in] Ignored.
lpTargetHandle
[out] Pointer to a variable that receives the value of the duplicate handle. This handle value is valid in the context of the target process.

lpTargetHandle can be NULL if dwOptions is DUPLICATE_CLOSE_SOURCE. With this combination of values, the source handle is closed and no new handle is created. You should use CloseHandle to achieve this same result.

dwDesiredAccess
[in] Ignored; use zero.
bInheritHandle
[in] Ignored; use FALSE.
dwOptions
[in] Specifies optional actions. This parameter can be zero, or the following value.
Value Meaning
DUPLICATE_CLOSE_SOURCE Closes the source handle. This occurs regardless of any error status returned.

Return Values

If the function succeeds, the return value is nonzero.

If the function fails, the return value is zero. To get extended error information, call GetLastError.

Remarks

DuplicateHandle can duplicate handles to the following types of objects.

Object Description
Directory The handle is returned by the CreateFile function.
Event The handle is returned by the CreateEvent or the OpenEvent function.
File The handle is returned by the CreateFile function.
Mutex The handle is returned by the CreateMutex or the OpenMutex function.
Semaphore The handle is returned by the CreateSemaphore or the OpenSemaphore function.
Thread The handle is returned by the CreateThread, or the GetCurrentThread function.
Timer The handle is returned by the CreateWaitableTimer or the OpenWaitableTimer function.

Note that DuplicateHandle should not be used to duplicate handles to I/O completion ports. In this case, no error is returned, but the duplicate handle cannot be used.

Requirements

Header: Declared in Winbase.h, for include use Xtl.h.
Import Library: Use Xapilib.lib

See Also

Handle and Object Functions | CloseHandle



Win32 Deltas

The hSourceProcessHandle and hTargetProcessHandle parameters are ignored. There is only one process—the title. GetCurrentProcess can be passed to these parameters if desired.

The dwDesiredAccess parameter is ignored. All handles have full access.

The bInheritHandle parameter is ignored. Child processes cannot be created. There is nothing that could inherit the handle.

The DUPLICATE_SAME_ACCESS flag of the dwOptions parameter is ignored. The flag is still defined and can be used, but all handles have the same full access as the source handle.

To make a call that will also work under Win32:

  • Use GetCurrentProcess as the argument for the hSourceProcessHandle and hTargetProcessHandle parameters.
  • Specify the DUPLICATE_SAME_ACCESS flag for dwOptions. In Win32, this means that the duplicate handle will have the same access as the source handle, and the dwDesiredAccess parameter will be ignored.
